question: What is a container and how does it differ from a virtual machine?
interviewerIntent: Fundamental Docker concept. Candidates should explain isolation, lightweight nature, and shared kernel.
answer: A container is a lightweight, isolated process that shares the host OS kernel but has its own filesystem, network, and process space via Linux namespaces and cgroups. A VM virtualizes the entire hardware stack and runs a full guest OS, consuming gigabytes of RAM and taking minutes to start. Containers share the host kernel, start in seconds, and use megabytes. The trade-off: containers are less isolated than VMs (shared kernel means a kernel exploit affects all containers on the host). For most application workloads, containers offer the right balance of isolation and efficiency.
shortAnswer: Container = isolated process sharing host kernel (namespaces + cgroups). VM = full guest OS on virtualized hardware. Containers: seconds to start, MBs of overhead. VMs: minutes, GBs.
keyPoints: Containers use Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount, UTS) for isolation
cgroups limit CPU, memory, and I/O
Shared kernel is the key difference from VMs
Container images are layered, immutable filesystems
Docker is not the only container runtime — containerd, podman also exist

followUps.question: What are Linux namespaces?
followUps.answerHint: Kernel feature that isolates system resources. PID namespace = isolated process tree. Network namespace = isolated network stack. Mount namespace = isolated filesystem view.

followUps.question: Are containers secure enough for production?
followUps.answerHint: Yes, with proper configuration: non-root user inside container, read-only filesystem where possible, resource limits, no privileged mode, image scanning.

mistakesToAvoid: Saying containers are 'like VMs but lighter' without explaining the kernel sharing
Thinking Docker invented containers — LXC and namespaces predate Docker
Assuming containers are fully isolated from the host

question: What happens when you run 'docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx'? Walk me through each flag.
interviewerIntent: Checks practical Docker CLI knowledge and understanding of port mapping and detached mode.
answer: docker run creates and starts a new container from the nginx image. -d runs it in detached mode (background), so the terminal is not attached to the container's stdout. -p 8080:80 maps port 8080 on the host to port 80 inside the container — traffic hitting localhost:8080 is forwarded to the container's port 80. nginx is the image name; Docker pulls it from Docker Hub if not cached locally. The result: an nginx web server accessible at http://localhost:8080 running in the background. To see it: docker ps. To stop it: docker stop <container-id>.
shortAnswer: -d = detached (background). -p host:container port mapping. nginx = image from Docker Hub. Result: nginx on localhost:8080 running in background.
keyPoints: -d detaches from stdio; container runs in background
-p hostPort:containerPort — host port is what you access externally
Image pulled from Docker Hub if not in local cache
docker ps shows running containers
docker logs <id> to see output from detached container

followUps.question: How do you run a container and get an interactive shell?
followUps.answerHint: docker run -it image /bin/sh — -i keeps stdin open, -t allocates a pseudo-TTY.

followUps.question: What is the difference between docker run and docker start?
followUps.answerHint: docker run creates a new container from an image. docker start restarts a stopped existing container.

mistakesToAvoid: Confusing -p 8080:80 order (host:container, not container:host)
Forgetting that -d means you need docker logs to see output
Thinking the container is deleted when stopped — use docker rm to delete

question: What is a Docker image and how are layers used?
interviewerIntent: Understanding of image layers is foundational for writing efficient Dockerfiles and understanding caching.
answer: A Docker image is a read-only, layered filesystem. Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new layer stacked on top of the previous one. Layers are content-addressed and cached — if a layer has not changed, Docker reuses it from cache instead of rebuilding. This means instruction order matters: put rarely-changing instructions (install dependencies) before frequently-changing ones (copy source code) to maximize cache hits. When you push or pull an image, only the changed layers transfer. A container adds a thin writable layer on top of the image layers — the image itself is never modified.
shortAnswer: Image = stacked read-only layers. Each Dockerfile instruction = one layer. Layers are cached. Order matters: stable instructions first to maximize cache reuse.
keyPoints: Each RUN, COPY, ADD instruction creates a layer
Layers are immutable and content-addressed (SHA256)
Cache invalidation: if a layer changes, all subsequent layers rebuild
Container = image layers + thin writable layer on top
docker history <image> shows layers and their sizes

followUps.question: How do you minimize image size using layers?
followUps.answerHint: Combine RUN commands with && to avoid intermediate layers. Use multi-stage builds. Use .dockerignore. Choose slim/alpine base images.

followUps.question: What is a dangling image?
followUps.answerHint: An image with no tag and no container using it — usually a previous build replaced by a newer one with the same tag. Clean with docker image prune.

mistakesToAvoid: Putting COPY . . before RUN npm install — invalidates dependency cache on every source change
Not using .dockerignore — copies node_modules or .git into the image
Creating many layers with separate RUN commands for related operations

question: What is a multi-stage build in Docker and why is it important?
interviewerIntent: Multi-stage builds are standard practice for production images. Candidates who don't know them produce bloated images with build tools included.
answer: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM instructions in one Dockerfile. Each stage can have its own base image and instructions. The final stage copies only the artifacts from previous stages — discarding build tools, source code, and intermediate files. Example: a Java app uses a Maven stage to compile (FROM maven:3.9 AS builder, RUN mvn package), then a second stage copies only the JAR (FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jre, COPY --from=builder /app/target/app.jar). The final image has only the JRE and the JAR — no Maven, no source code. This dramatically reduces image size (from ~600MB to ~200MB) and attack surface.
shortAnswer: Multiple FROM stages. Build stage compiles/installs. Final stage copies only artifacts. Eliminates build tools from production image. Reduces size and attack surface.
keyPoints: Each FROM starts a new stage with its own filesystem
COPY --from=stageName copies artifacts between stages
Only the final stage ends up in the output image
Reduces image size significantly (build tools not included)
Reduces attack surface (no compiler, no build scripts in production)

followUps.question: Can you have more than two stages?
followUps.answerHint: Yes. Common pattern: test stage (run tests), build stage (compile), final stage (production image). Stages can also be used for conditional logic.

followUps.question: How do you build only a specific stage?
followUps.answerHint: docker build --target stageName . — useful for running tests in CI without producing the final image.

mistakesToAvoid: Including Maven/npm/Gradle in the production image
Copying the entire source code into the final image
Using the same large base image for build and runtime

question: What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile?
interviewerIntent: Frequently confused. Understanding the difference is necessary for writing flexible, production-ready images.
answer: ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that always runs — it is not overridden by arguments to docker run. CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT (or a default command if no ENTRYPOINT is set). When both are used: ENTRYPOINT is the process, CMD is the default args. docker run myimage --verbose would replace CMD but not ENTRYPOINT. A common pattern: ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"] with CMD ["--spring.profiles.active=prod"] as default profile. To override ENTRYPOINT: docker run --entrypoint /bin/sh myimage. For scripts, ENTRYPOINT with exec form ['./entrypoint.sh'] is preferred to preserve signal handling.
shortAnswer: ENTRYPOINT = always-run executable (not overridden by docker run args). CMD = default args to ENTRYPOINT or default command. Both in exec form [] for signal handling.
keyPoints: ENTRYPOINT is not replaced by docker run arguments
CMD IS replaced by docker run arguments
Use exec form (["cmd", "arg"]) not shell form for signal handling
Shell form wraps in /bin/sh -c, which prevents PID 1 signal propagation
ENTRYPOINT + CMD together = executable + default args

followUps.question: Why does exec form matter for signal handling?
followUps.answerHint: Shell form makes /bin/sh PID 1, which may not forward SIGTERM to the real process. Exec form makes your process PID 1, receiving signals directly — critical for graceful shutdown.

followUps.question: When would you use only CMD without ENTRYPOINT?
followUps.answerHint: For general-purpose images where the user might run different commands (e.g., a development utility image). ENTRYPOINT is better when the image has one clear purpose.

mistakesToAvoid: Shell form for ENTRYPOINT — breaks SIGTERM handling
Putting the full command in ENTRYPOINT with no CMD — makes it inflexible
Confusing which one docker run arguments replace

question: What is Docker Compose and how do you use it for a multi-service application?
interviewerIntent: Docker Compose is standard for local development. Checks practical multi-service orchestration knowledge.
answer: Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container applications via a docker-compose.yml file. Each service defines its image or build context, environment variables, ports, volumes, and dependencies. 'docker compose up' starts all services; 'docker compose down' stops and removes containers. Services communicate by service name within the compose network — app can reach db at postgres://db:5432. Key features: depends_on for startup order (but not readiness — use healthchecks), volumes for persistent data, networks for isolation between service groups, and override files (docker-compose.override.yml) for local dev differences.
shortAnswer: docker-compose.yml defines services, networks, volumes. Services reach each other by name. docker compose up/down. depends_on for order, healthcheck for readiness.
keyPoints: Services communicate via service name on the default network
depends_on controls start order but not readiness
Use healthcheck + condition: service_healthy for true readiness
volumes: persist data outside container lifecycle
docker compose up -d for detached, docker compose logs -f to follow

followUps.question: How do you share environment variables across services?
followUps.answerHint: env_file to load from .env file, or environment: block inline. Docker Compose automatically reads .env in the same directory for variable substitution.

followUps.question: How is Docker Compose different from Kubernetes?
followUps.answerHint: Compose is for local development and simple single-host deployments. Kubernetes is for production multi-host orchestration with scaling, rolling updates, and self-healing.

mistakesToAvoid: Using depends_on without healthcheck for readiness-sensitive services
Hardcoding passwords in docker-compose.yml — use env_file or secrets
Not defining volumes for databases — losing data on docker compose down

question: How does Docker networking work? What are the main network drivers?
interviewerIntent: Networking is a common source of issues in containerized apps. Checks understanding of bridge, host, and overlay networks.
answer: Docker provides several network drivers. Bridge (default): creates a virtual network on the host; containers on the same bridge network communicate by IP or name. Each docker-compose project gets its own bridge network automatically. Host: container shares the host network stack directly — no port mapping needed, but no network isolation. None: no networking. Overlay: multi-host networking for Docker Swarm. In practice for development: compose creates a bridge network; services reach each other by service name via Docker's embedded DNS. For production on Kubernetes: Docker networking is replaced by the CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, etc).
shortAnswer: Bridge (default, container-to-container by name), Host (shares host network, no isolation), Overlay (multi-host Swarm). Compose auto-creates bridge; services talk by service name.
keyPoints: Bridge: isolated virtual network, containers talk by name
Host: shares host network — no port mapping, no isolation
Compose creates a project-scoped bridge network automatically
Docker embedded DNS resolves service names within a network
Containers on different networks cannot communicate by default

followUps.question: How do you connect a container to multiple networks?
followUps.answerHint: Assign multiple networks in docker-compose.yml under the service's networks key, or docker network connect for running containers.

followUps.question: When would you use --network host?
followUps.answerHint: When the container needs to access host-only services or when port mapping overhead matters. Avoid in production — no network isolation.

mistakesToAvoid: Using localhost inside a container to reach another container — use service name
Exposing all ports with -p without considering security
Assuming containers on different compose projects can communicate by name

question: How do you optimize a Docker image for a Java Spring Boot application?
interviewerIntent: Practical question combining Dockerfile best practices with Java-specific knowledge. Shows production readiness.
answer: Key optimizations: (1) Multi-stage build — Maven/Gradle stage to build the JAR, JRE-only final stage (eclipse-temurin:17-jre instead of JDK). (2) Layer the JAR — Spring Boot layered JARs split into dependencies, spring-boot-loader, snapshot-dependencies, and application layers; dependencies change rarely so they're cached. (3) Use a non-root user — RUN addgroup -S spring && adduser -S spring -G spring, USER spring. (4) JVM flags for containers: -XX:+UseContainerSupport (default in Java 11+) respects cgroup memory limits; set -Xmx explicitly or use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75. (5) COPY .dockerignore to exclude target/, .git, etc.
shortAnswer: Multi-stage (JRE only), layered JAR for cache, non-root user, -XX:MaxRAMPercentage for container memory, .dockerignore to exclude build artifacts.
keyPoints: eclipse-temurin:17-jre saves ~200MB vs JDK image
Spring Boot layered JARs: separate slow-changing deps from fast-changing code
Non-root USER is a security best practice
-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 prevents OOMKilled by container memory limit
.dockerignore prevents target/ and .git from invalidating COPY cache

followUps.question: What is GraalVM native image and when is it worth it for Spring Boot?
followUps.answerHint: Compiles to native binary — sub-second startup, low memory. Worth it for serverless/short-lived workloads. Long-running services benefit less and lose dynamic features.

followUps.question: How do you set JVM heap size in a Kubernetes pod?
followUps.answerHint: Set resources.limits.memory in the pod spec and use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 so JVM reads the cgroup limit rather than host RAM.

mistakesToAvoid: Using JDK image in production — includes compiler and tools not needed at runtime
Not setting memory limits — JVM uses host RAM by default, causing OOMKilled
Running as root inside the container

question: What is .dockerignore and why is it important?
interviewerIntent: Often overlooked but critical for cache efficiency and security. Missing .dockerignore is a common production mistake.
answer: .dockerignore works like .gitignore — it lists files and directories to exclude from the build context sent to the Docker daemon. Without it, the entire project directory is sent, including node_modules, target/, .git, test data, and local configs. This causes problems: large build context slows the build, COPY . . includes unwanted files in the image, and any change in excluded files (like .git) invalidates the COPY cache layer. Typical .dockerignore entries: .git, target/, node_modules/, *.md, .env, test/, .idea/.
shortAnswer: .dockerignore excludes files from build context. Without it: slow builds, bloated images, cache invalidation on .git changes. Always exclude .git, target/, node_modules/.
keyPoints: Reduces build context size sent to Docker daemon
Prevents sensitive files (.env, credentials) from entering image
Prevents .git changes from invalidating COPY cache
Same syntax as .gitignore
Place in same directory as Dockerfile

followUps.question: Can .dockerignore exclude the Dockerfile itself?
followUps.answerHint: Yes, but Docker always includes the Dockerfile and .dockerignore regardless of rules in .dockerignore.

followUps.question: What happens if you COPY a file that's in .dockerignore?
followUps.answerHint: The COPY instruction fails with 'no such file or directory' — the file was excluded from the build context before Docker processes the Dockerfile.

mistakesToAvoid: Forgetting .dockerignore and including node_modules/ in the image
Not excluding .git — causes cache miss on every commit
Excluding files needed by the build

question: How do you manage persistent data in Docker? What are volumes vs bind mounts?
interviewerIntent: Data persistence is a fundamental concern for stateful services. Checks understanding of Docker storage options.
answer: Docker containers are ephemeral — data written inside is lost when the container is removed. Two main persistence mechanisms: Volumes are managed by Docker, stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes/, and are portable between containers. They're the preferred way to persist database data. Bind mounts map a host directory into the container — changes on host reflect immediately inside. Preferred for development (mounting source code for hot reload). Key difference: volumes are Docker-managed (docker volume ls, docker volume prune), bind mounts are host-path-dependent. For databases in production: named volume. For development config or code: bind mount.
shortAnswer: Volume = Docker-managed, portable, preferred for production data. Bind mount = host path mapped into container, preferred for dev (live code reload). Containers are ephemeral without one.
keyPoints: Named volumes: docker volume create, referenced by name in compose
Bind mounts: absolute host path mapped to container path
tmpfs mount: in-memory, not persisted — for sensitive temporary data
Volumes survive docker compose down; use -v flag to also remove volumes
Never store production database data only in container filesystem

followUps.question: How do you backup a Docker volume?
followUps.answerHint: Mount the volume in a temporary container and use tar: docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/backup.tar.gz /data.

followUps.question: What are the performance implications of bind mounts on macOS?
followUps.answerHint: macOS bind mounts are slow due to osxfs/VirtioFS translation layer. For large node_modules, use a named volume instead.

mistakesToAvoid: Assuming container data persists after docker rm
Using bind mounts in production — host path dependency is fragile
Forgetting to define volumes for database containers in compose

question: How do you handle secrets and environment-specific configuration in Docker Compose?
interviewerIntent: Security-conscious candidates avoid hardcoded secrets. This distinguishes developers who have shipped to production from those who only develop locally.
answer: Best practices: use env_file to load from a .env file not committed to git. Docker Compose reads .env automatically for variable substitution (${DB_PASSWORD}). For production, Docker Secrets (Swarm) or Kubernetes Secrets mount secrets as files — read by the app from /run/secrets/. Never hardcode credentials in docker-compose.yml. For different environments: use override files — docker-compose.yml as base, docker-compose.override.yml for local dev, docker-compose.prod.yml for production (docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up). This avoids duplicating the full config per environment.
shortAnswer: env_file for .env not in git. Variable substitution with ${VAR}. Override files for env-specific config. Never hardcode secrets. Production: Docker Secrets or K8s Secrets as files.
keyPoints: .env file loaded automatically — add to .gitignore
Variable substitution: ${VARIABLE:-default} with fallback
Override files: base + environment-specific layer
docker-compose.override.yml merged automatically with docker compose up
Docker Secrets mount as files — more secure than env vars for sensitive data

followUps.question: Why are environment variables less secure than file-based secrets?
followUps.answerHint: Env vars can be read by any process in the container, appear in docker inspect output, and can leak into logs. Files with restricted permissions are harder to accidentally expose.

followUps.question: How do you validate that required environment variables are set?
followUps.answerHint: Use ${REQUIRED_VAR:?Error message} syntax in docker-compose.yml — Compose fails with the error message if the variable is unset.

mistakesToAvoid: Committing .env files to git
Hardcoding passwords in docker-compose.yml
Duplicating the full config in a docker-compose.prod.yml instead of using overrides

question: What is image scanning and why is it important in a CI/CD pipeline?
interviewerIntent: Security awareness in containerized environments. Shows production maturity.
answer: Image scanning analyzes Docker images for known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) in base images, installed packages, and application dependencies. Tools: Trivy (open source, fast), Snyk, Grype, Docker Scout. In CI/CD, scanning runs after build and blocks deployment if critical/high vulnerabilities exist. Key practices: use minimal base images (distroless, alpine) to reduce attack surface, regularly rebuild images even without code changes (base image vulnerabilities), pin base image versions with digest (FROM ubuntu:22.04@sha256:abc...) to prevent unexpected updates, and never run as root. A layered approach: scan in CI, also scan in registry on push.
shortAnswer: Image scanning finds CVEs in base images and dependencies. Run Trivy/Snyk in CI. Block deploys on critical CVEs. Use minimal base images, pin with digest, non-root user.
keyPoints: Trivy and Grype are popular open-source scanners
Scan on every build and also in the registry
Distroless images minimize attack surface (no shell, no package manager)
Pin base images with SHA256 digest for reproducibility
Regularly rebuild even without app changes to pick up base image patches

followUps.question: What is a distroless image?
followUps.answerHint: Images with only the runtime (JRE, Python, etc.) and no OS utilities — no shell, no package manager. Dramatically reduces attack surface. gcr.io/distroless/ images.

followUps.question: How do you handle false positives in image scanning?
followUps.answerHint: Document and suppress known false positives in .trivyignore or scanner config with a justification. Review and expire suppressions periodically.

mistakesToAvoid: Building images once and never rebuilding — base image vulnerabilities accumulate
Using :latest tag — unpredictable, not reproducible
Ignoring high severity findings because they seem theoretical

question: How do you debug a running container or investigate why a container keeps crashing?
interviewerIntent: Practical troubleshooting skill. Candidates who can debug containers independently are much more valuable in production.
answer: For a running container: docker exec -it <id> /bin/sh for an interactive shell. docker logs <id> or docker logs -f <id> for streaming logs. docker inspect <id> for metadata, environment variables, mounts, and network config. docker stats <id> for live CPU/memory usage. For a crashing container: docker logs <id> shows the last output before crash. docker inspect <id> shows exit code and error. If the container exits immediately: override the entrypoint to get a shell — docker run --entrypoint /bin/sh -it myimage. Check OOMKilled in docker inspect State.OOMKilled (true = memory limit hit). Use --restart no to prevent automatic restart during debugging.
shortAnswer: Running: docker exec -it for shell, docker logs for output, docker stats for resources. Crashing: check exit code via inspect, override entrypoint to get shell, check OOMKilled.
keyPoints: docker exec -it <id> /bin/sh — interactive shell in running container
docker logs --tail 100 -f — last 100 lines + follow
docker inspect State.ExitCode and State.OOMKilled
docker run --entrypoint /bin/sh to debug containers that exit immediately
docker events — real-time event stream from Docker daemon

followUps.question: What if the container image has no shell (distroless)?
followUps.answerHint: Use docker cp to copy files out. Use kubectl debug (in K8s) with an ephemeral debug container. Or temporarily use a non-distroless image for debugging.

followUps.question: How do you investigate a container that was OOMKilled?
followUps.answerHint: docker inspect State.OOMKilled = true confirms it. Increase memory limit or optimize the application. Use -Xmx for JVM apps to set explicit heap limit.

mistakesToAvoid: Deleting the crashed container before collecting logs
Not checking OOMKilled when a container exits unexpectedly
Assuming the error is in the app without checking docker logs first

question: What is the difference between COPY and ADD in a Dockerfile?
interviewerIntent: Simple but important distinction. ADD has hidden behaviors that can cause security and reproducibility issues.
answer: COPY simply copies files from the build context into the image. ADD does everything COPY does, plus: it can fetch remote URLs (ADD http://example.com/file.tar.gz /app/) and it automatically extracts tar archives. The Docker best practice is to always use COPY unless you specifically need the tar extraction feature. ADD with URLs is discouraged because it cannot be cached effectively and introduces remote dependencies into the build. If you need to download a file, use RUN curl or RUN wget instead — it's explicit and cacheable. COPY is predictable; ADD has magic behavior that surprises people.
shortAnswer: COPY = simple file copy. ADD = COPY + URL fetch + tar extraction. Always prefer COPY. Use RUN curl for downloads. ADD's magic behavior is a source of surprises.
keyPoints: COPY is explicit and predictable
ADD auto-extracts .tar.gz, .tar.bz2, etc.
ADD with URLs bypasses the build cache
Prefer COPY for files, RUN curl/wget for downloads
Official Dockerfile linter (hadolint) warns about ADD misuse

followUps.question: Can COPY handle glob patterns?
followUps.answerHint: Yes — COPY src/*.jar /app/ copies all JARs from src. Uses Go filepath.Match patterns.

followUps.question: How do you copy files from a previous stage using COPY?
followUps.answerHint: COPY --from=stageName /path/in/stage /path/in/current — the --from flag targets a named stage.

mistakesToAvoid: Using ADD when COPY suffices
Using ADD with a URL — not cacheable, non-reproducible
Expecting ADD to recursively copy directories like cp -r (it does, but COPY is clearer)

question: How does health checking work in Docker and why is it critical for orchestration?
interviewerIntent: Health checks bridge Docker and Kubernetes readiness. Candidates who understand them write more reliable service deployments.
answer: HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile defines a command that Docker runs periodically inside the container to determine if it's healthy. Syntax: HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --retries=3 CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1. Status: starting (initial), healthy, unhealthy. Docker Compose depends_on with condition: service_healthy waits for a service to be healthy before starting dependents — solving the 'database not ready' race condition. In Kubernetes, liveness and readiness probes replace the Docker HEALTHCHECK for pod scheduling and traffic routing. Key: health check should test actual readiness (can the app serve requests?), not just that the process is running.
shortAnswer: HEALTHCHECK runs a command periodically. Status: starting/healthy/unhealthy. In Compose: depends_on condition: service_healthy. In K8s: replaced by liveness/readiness probes.
keyPoints: --interval (how often), --timeout (max wait), --retries (failures before unhealthy)
depends_on condition: service_healthy waits for actual readiness
Health endpoint should test real readiness (DB connection, required services)
Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/health is perfect for this
K8s liveness vs readiness: liveness restarts unhealthy pods, readiness removes from LB

followUps.question: What is the difference between liveness and readiness in Kubernetes?
followUps.answerHint: Liveness: is the app in a broken state that requires restart? Readiness: is the app ready to serve traffic? An app can be live but not ready (warming up, downstream unavailable).

followUps.question: What should a health check NOT do?
followUps.answerHint: Heavy queries, external HTTP calls with no timeout, anything that takes >5s. A slow health check causes false unhealthy status and unnecessary restarts.

mistakesToAvoid: Health check that only verifies the process is running, not that it can serve requests
Expensive health checks (full DB query, external HTTP) — they run every 30s
Not using service_healthy in depends_on and wondering why app starts before DB is ready
